# AgentLedger

> The trust layer for AI coding agents. AgentLedger verifies an agent's completion claims against real process exit codes instead of self-reports, blocks writes to protected files before they reach disk, and records every action to an append-only, SHA-256 hash-chained ledger. It runs as a Claude Code plugin (the primary surface) and as a standalone CLI orchestrator, entirely on the local machine â€” no hosting, no telemetry, no account.

AgentLedger observes a Claude Code session through five hooks and records structured events to `.agentledger/ledger.jsonl`. A trust score â€” verified-true claims divided by total verified claims â€” is tracked across sessions. This file is written for machine readers; every claim below is meant to match the actual behavior of the code.

## What it enforces (honest model)

The mechanisms below carry different guarantees. Do not overstate them.

- Edit/Write to protected paths â€” PREVENTED before disk. The PreToolUse hook inspects Edit and Write tool calls; a target matching `blockedFiles` (default globs: `**/.env`, `**/secrets.*`, `**/*.pem`, `**/*.key`) is rejected with exit code 2, so the file is never written. `warnFiles` patterns warn but do not block.
- Bash-driven file changes â€” DETECTED, not prevented. A shell command (for example `echo secret > .env`) does not go through the Edit/Write tool path and is NOT blocked before disk. It is caught after the fact at SessionEnd by a `git diff` boundary check against the declared boundaries, and recorded as a boundary violation. The guarantee for arbitrary shell writes is detection, not prevention.
- Completion claims â€” VERIFIED against real exit codes. The Stop hook scans the assistant turn for a completion claim ("tests pass", "fixed the bug", "done"); on a match it runs the configured `testCommand` (default `npm test`) and records CLAIM_VERIFIED or CLAIM_FALSIFIED based on the exit code. The agent's self-report is logged but never trusted to mark work complete.

## Hook lifecycle

- SessionStart: creates `.agentledger/`, validates the hash chain, prints the trust banner, starts a local dashboard at http://localhost:4242.
- PreToolUse: blocks Edit/Write to `blockedFiles` before disk (exit code 2); warns on `warnFiles`.
- PostToolUse: records each Edit/Write/Bash/Read event to the ledger; tracks the read:edit ratio.
- Stop: detects completion claims in the turn and runs the real test command, producing a verified or falsified result.
- SessionEnd: runs the `git diff` boundary check, runs the test command, writes the session summary, updates the trust score.

## Ledger

Append-only JSONL at `.agentledger/ledger.jsonl`. Every event carries `hash` and `previous_hash`, a rolling SHA-256 of `previous_hash` concatenated with the serialized event payload. The chain breaks if any event is altered, and it is validated on each session start. Only the orchestrator appends to the ledger; workers return structured results and never write to it directly. Replay reconstructs run state from the event log â€” it does not re-execute agents to reproduce identical output.

## Install (works today)

Run these two commands inside Claude Code:

```
/plugin marketplace add aryansahni30/AgentLedger
/plugin install agentledger@agentledger
```

This reads the repository's marketplace manifest and installs the `agentledger-plugin` package from npm, wiring all five hooks automatically â€” no `settings.json` editing. The ledger and a default `.agentledger/config.json` are created on first run, after which every session in the project is observed and scored.

## Skills (invoke inside Claude Code)

- `/trust`: trust score breakdown, recent false claims, and accuracy trend.
- `/ledger`: recent ledger events and run state for this project.
- `/verify`: manually run the boundary check plus test command mid-session.
- `/audit`: trust score, risk assessment, and compliance/governance report.
- `/handoff`: generate a handoff document to resume the work in a new session.

## CLI (optional, standalone)

AgentLedger also ships an orchestrator that is independent of Claude Code. `agentledger run "<request>"` compiles the request into a task graph and executes tasks sequentially in dependency order; each task runs inside its own git worktree scoped to its allowed files, and a verifier diffs the task branch against the declared boundaries and runs the test command before any patch merges. Commands: `init`, `run`, `ledger view`, `verify`, `replay`.

## Configuration

`.agentledger/config.json` is auto-created on first run; all fields are optional. Defaults: `blockedFiles` = `["**/.env", "**/secrets.*", "**/*.pem", "**/*.key"]`; `warnFiles` = `["**/migrations/**", "**/auth/**", "package.json", "**/middleware.*"]`; `testCommand` = `"npm test"`; `testTimeout` = `30000`; `claimDetection` = `true`; `dashboardPort` = `4242`.

## Links

- [Repository](https://github.com/aryansahni30/AgentLedger)
- [README](https://github.com/aryansahni30/AgentLedger/blob/main/README.md)
- [npm package: agentledger-plugin](https://www.npmjs.com/package/agentledger-plugin)
- [Landing page](https://aryansahni30.github.io/AgentLedger/)
- [License (MIT)](https://github.com/aryansahni30/AgentLedger/blob/main/LICENSE)

## Not

- No cloud, no telemetry, no account; everything runs locally.
- Tool restrictions (`allowedTools`) are audit-only metadata, not a technical sandbox â€” there is no mechanism that prevents an agent with shell access from invoking out-of-scope tools; the ledger audits this after the fact.
- AgentLedger does not host, transmit, or store data off the machine.
